question.text: Priya wants to send her daughter abroad for higher education. The current cost of the programme is Rs. 5,00,000. If education inflation is 10% per annum, what will be the cost of the programme 8 years from now?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Using the Future Value formula: FV = PV x (1 + r)^n = Rs. 5,00,000 x (1.10)^8 = Rs. 5,00,000 x 2.14359 = Rs. 10,71,794. Education inflation at 10% doubles costs roughly every 7.2 years (Rule of 72), so after 8 years the cost more than doubles. **The Trap:** Rs. 9,00,000 results from using simple interest (5,00,000 + 5,00,000 x 10% x 8 = 9,00,000) instead of compound interest. Many candidates confuse simple and compound interest in time-value calculations. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-A (Investment Adviser Level 1) tests basic time-value-of-money concepts. Series V-A (Mutual Fund Distributors) covers goal-based planning where education inflation is a critical input. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Education inflation in India consistently runs 8-12% — nearly double general CPI inflation. Always use a separate, higher inflation rate for education goals. Start SIPs early because compounding works against you on the cost side and for you on the savings side.
